project.name: jobsync
project.description: Self-hosted job application tracker built with Next.js 15, Prisma (SQLite), and Shadcn UI. Includes EURES/ESCO integration for European job discovery, a Connector/Module ACL architecture, CRM core, multi-channel notification dispatch, and a public API v1. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what JobSync is: a self-hosted job-application tracker built on Next.js 15, Prisma/SQLite, React 19, and NextAuth. It introduces the headline capabilities — the Connector/Module ACL, the CRM core, multi-channel notifications, EURES/ESCO integration, and a public API — and walks through self-hosting and AI configuration (Ollama/Deepseek). Read this first to get the mental map the rest of the tour fills in.

tour.title: Boot Sequence & Entry Points
tour.description: JobSync has two boot surfaces. instrumentation.ts is the server-startup hook: its register() function wires up event consumers and starts the automation scheduler, health scheduler, CRM cron, and retention cron — so background work begins the moment the server comes up. layout.tsx and page.tsx are the request-time entry points of the App Router: the root layout establishes locale, theme, and error capture, while the root route redirects users to the dashboard, sign-in, or sign-up. This step shows how the app comes alive both as a process and as a web request.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js calls the exported register() in instrumentation.ts once per server process before handling any request — the idiomatic place to start schedulers and subscribe event consumers in the App Router.

tour.title: Authentication Core
tour.description: Building on the redirect logic from Step 2, auth.ts is the central NextAuth instance. It wires a credentials provider with bcrypt password verification and exports the auth/handlers/signIn/signOut primitives reused across pages, server actions, and route handlers. With 29 inbound dependencies, this is the gatekeeper every protected surface routes through, which is why it appears so early in the tour.

tour.title: The Data Model
tour.description: schema.prisma is the authoritative SQLite data model and the single source of truth for every persisted entity: Job, Automation, Company, Profile/Resume, CRM contacts, Tasks, and Notifications. Understanding these tables and their relationships is essential before reading the code, because the domain models and repositories in later steps map directly onto this schema. The accompanying migration history records how the schema evolved feature-by-feature.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma migrations are ordered, append-only SQL snapshots. Each migration applies incremental, forward-only changes, letting the database schema evolve in lockstep with the code while preserving a full audit trail of structural changes.

tour.title: Database Client & Domain Models
tour.description: db.ts is the Prisma client singleton — it survives Hot Module Reload by caching the instance on globalThis, so dev servers don't exhaust connections. Around it sit the domain models that give the raw tables meaning: job.model.ts defines the Job aggregate's value objects and view models, while actionResult.ts defines the typed ActionResult<T> contract that every server action returns. These models are the vocabulary the entire codebase speaks in.
tour.languageLesson: ActionResult<T> is a discriminated union: a success branch carries typed data while a failure branch carries an error, so callers must narrow on a discriminant field before accessing the payload — making error handling exhaustive at compile time.

tour.title: Server Actions as Repositories
tour.description: JobSync follows a DDD-flavored pattern where files under src/actions act as repositories: they are the only sanctioned path between the UI and the database, each returning the ActionResult<T> contract from Step 5. job.actions.ts (17 exported actions) is the workhorse for the Job aggregate, and automation.actions.ts manages the Automation aggregate that drives job discovery. This is where authorization, validation, and persistence converge.
tour.languageLesson: A file beginning with the 'use server' directive marks its exports as Next.js Server Actions — RPC endpoints callable directly from client components, executed only on the server. JobSync layers a Repository discipline on top so all data access is centralized and typed.

tour.title: The Connector / Module ACL
tour.description: This is JobSync's signature architecture. ADR-004 establishes the App-to-Connector-to-Module Anti-Corruption Layer that isolates the app from external integrations (job-discovery, ai-provider, data-enrichment, reference-data). manifest.ts defines the shared-kernel manifest types every module declares — credentials, health, resilience, i18n — and registry.ts is the unified ModuleRegistry that registers modules, instantiates them via factories, and tracks their health and circuit-breaker state. Read the ADR first for the 'why', then the code for the 'how'.

tour.title: The Job-Discovery Pipeline
tour.description: runner.ts is the concrete payoff of the connector architecture from Step 7 — the highest fan-out orchestrator in the lib layer. It drives the full job-discovery pipeline end to end: search across connectors, dedup, blacklist filtering, enrichment, AI matching against the user's resume, staging of vacancies, and finalizing the run with graceful degradation when a module is unhealthy. This single file ties the connector ACL, the Automation aggregate, and the AI provider together into one runnable flow.

tour.title: Domain Events & the Event Bus
tour.description: JobSync is event-driven internally. event-types.ts declares all 29 domain event types with strongly typed payloads plus the createEvent() factory used at every emit site, and index.ts is the barrel exposing the eventBus singleton and the fire-and-forget emitEvent helper. The schedulers and actions from earlier steps emit these events; the next step shows who listens. This decoupling lets new reactions be added without touching the code that produces the events.
tour.languageLesson: The TypedEventBus uses a discriminated union of event types so that emitEvent and subscribers are type-checked: subscribing to a specific event narrows its payload automatically, preventing mismatched handlers at compile time.

tour.title: Multi-Channel Notification Dispatch
tour.description: This is the primary consumer of the event bus from Step 9. notification-dispatcher.ts subscribes to 11 domain events and builds a dispatch context, while channel-router.ts fans each notification out across the email, in-app, push, and webhook channels. Together they turn internal domain events (a new vacancy staged, an automation completed) into user-facing notifications, with the channel abstraction making it easy to add new delivery mechanisms.

tour.title: EURES / ESCO Integration & Reference Data
tour.description: JobSync integrates the EU's EURES job network and ESCO occupation taxonomy as reference-data connectors. The ESCO and EURES proxy route handlers authenticate the user and broker upstream occupation, language, and location lookups, while the EURES comboboxes in the automation wizard let users pick standardized occupations, languages-with-proficiency, and NUTS regions. This shows how an external classification system flows through the connector ACL into the UI as structured, localized inputs.

tour.title: Public API v1
tour.description: Beyond the internal server actions, JobSync exposes a versioned public REST API under src/app/api/v1 for programmatic access to jobs. The collection route handles GET/POST on /jobs, while the item routes cover GET/PATCH/DELETE on a single job plus dedicated status-change and notes endpoints. These handlers ultimately call the same action-layer repositories from Step 6, so the public API and the UI share one consistent domain core.
tour.languageLesson: In the App Router, a route.ts file exports named functions per HTTP verb (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE). The framework maps the file path to the URL and dispatches by method, so one file cleanly defines a REST resource's full surface.

tour.title: Specifications & Testing Discipline
tour.description: JobSync is spec-driven: Allium specifications under specs/ capture intended behavior independently of the implementation — crm.allium defines the CRM bounded context and action-result.allium pins down the ActionResult contract you met in Step 5. These specs anchor the project's extensive Jest and Playwright test suites and document the GDPR, CRM, and connector obligations the code must satisfy. End the tour here to understand how the team keeps behavior, documentation, and code aligned as the system grows.
